clinicalStem: A 38-year-old woman at 16 weeks gestation attends her antenatal clinic. Her triple screen shows low alpha-fetoprotein, low unconjugated estriol, and elevated human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG). She has no family history of chromosomal abnormalities. The obstetrician discusses the risk of a chromosomal disorder. Which single investigation would BEST confirm the suspected diagnosis in the fetus?
options: Maternal serum alpha-fetoprotein repeat at 20 weeks
Detailed fetal anomaly ultrasound scan only
Chorionic villus sampling for FISH of chromosome 21
explanation: Amniocentesis for fetal karyotype analysis is the gold standard confirmatory test for chromosomal abnormalities including trisomy 21 (Down syndrome). The triple screen pattern (low AFP, low unconjugated estriol, elevated hCG) is the classical biochemical profile associated with trisomy 21. Karyotyping provides a complete chromosomal picture and confirms the exact diagnosis.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — amniocentesis with karyotype is the definitive diagnostic test at 16 weeks gestation.
Repeating maternal AFP alone does not confirm a chromosomal diagnosis; it screens for neural tube defects.
Ultrasound can identify soft markers (nuchal fold, short femur) but is not diagnostic of trisomy 21.
FISH for chromosome 21 is rapid but does not provide a full karyotype; it may miss other chromosomal anomalies present simultaneously.
Cell-free fetal DNA (NIPT) is a highly sensitive screening test but is not diagnostic; a positive result requires invasive confirmation.
clinicalPearl: Triple screen showing low AFP + low unconjugated estriol + high hCG strongly suggests trisomy 21; amniocentesis for karyotype is the definitive confirmatory investigation.
keyClue: Triple screen pattern: low AFP, low uE3, high hCG in a 38-year-old (advanced maternal age).
commonExamTrap: Choosing NIPT as the definitive test — it is screening, not diagnosis; karyotype is required for confirmation.
missedConcept: Distinction between screening tests (triple screen, NIPT, ultrasound) and diagnostic tests (karyotype via amniocentesis) for chromosomal abnormalities.

clinicalStem: A 15-year-old girl is referred by her family physician for primary amenorrhea. She is 142 cm tall (significantly below the 3rd centile for her age). On examination she has a low posterior hairline, a broad chest with widely spaced nipples, and cubitus valgus. Her secondary sexual characteristics are absent. Blood pressure is 130/85 mmHg. What is the MOST LIKELY diagnosis?
options: Constitutional delay of growth and puberty
explanation: Turner syndrome (45,X monosomy) classically presents with short stature, primary amenorrhea, streak gonads, webbed neck or low posterior hairline, shield chest with wide-spaced nipples, cubitus valgus, and hypertension from coarctation of the aorta or renal anomalies. The combination of all these features in a phenotypic female with absent secondary sexual development is diagnostic until proven otherwise.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — the constellation of dysmorphic features (low hairline, shield chest, cubitus valgus), short stature, absent puberty, and hypertension is classic for Turner syndrome.
Constitutional delay is common but does NOT cause dysmorphic features; puberty is merely delayed, not absent, and growth velocity is preserved relative to bone age.
Hypothyroidism causes growth failure but not the specific somatic features seen here (cubitus valgus, wide-spaced nipples, low hairline).
Noonan syndrome affects both sexes, features are similar but include normal karyotype (XX or XY), pulmonary stenosis, and ptosis; primary amenorrhea is not characteristic.
Isolated GH deficiency causes short stature but not the dysmorphic features, absent puberty, or hypertension seen in this patient.
clinicalPearl: In any phenotypic girl with short stature, primary amenorrhea, and somatic anomalies (shield chest, cubitus valgus, low hairline), Turner syndrome must be excluded with karyotyping.
keyClue: Short stature + primary amenorrhea + low posterior hairline + shield chest + cubitus valgus in a 15-year-old girl.
commonExamTrap: Choosing constitutional delay — it does not produce dysmorphic features and puberty is delayed, not absent.
missedConcept: Turner syndrome can present in adolescence with primary amenorrhea and subtle dysmorphic features that may be overlooked in a routine exam.

subtopic: Diagnosis in a child with intellectual disability
clinicalStem: A 7-year-old boy is brought by his mother due to learning difficulties and behavioural problems. His teachers report he is hyperactive and has difficulty with social interactions. On examination he has a long face, prominent ears, and macroorchidism. His maternal uncle also has intellectual disability. IQ testing reveals moderate intellectual disability. Which investigation will BEST confirm the suspected diagnosis?
options: Southern blot or PCR for FMR1 CGG repeat expansion
explanation: Fragile X syndrome is caused by a CGG trinucleotide repeat expansion in the FMR1 gene on the X chromosome. Southern blot or PCR-based molecular testing is the definitive diagnostic investigation. Classic features include intellectual disability, long face, large ears, macroorchidism (post-pubertal but may be present in prepubertal boys), and X-linked inheritance through the maternal line.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — Southern blot or PCR for FMR1 CGG repeat expansion directly detects the molecular cause of Fragile X syndrome.
Standard karyotype may show the fragile site at Xq27.3 under special culture conditions but is insensitive and not the test of choice; molecular testing has replaced it.
Array CGH detects copy number variants but will miss trinucleotide repeat expansions, which are a dynamic mutation not a deletion/duplication.
Brain MRI is not diagnostic for Fragile X; it may show non-specific findings but does not confirm the genetic diagnosis.
Serum testosterone and FSH would assess testicular function but have no role in diagnosing Fragile X syndrome.
clinicalPearl: Fragile X is the most common inherited cause of intellectual disability in males; long face, large ears, macroorchidism, and X-linked family history should prompt FMR1 molecular testing.
keyClue: Long face + large ears + macroorchidism + X-linked inheritance pattern (maternal uncle affected) in a boy with intellectual disability.
commonExamTrap: Ordering a standard karyotype — it was historically used but molecular FMR1 testing is the current gold standard.
missedConcept: Fragile X is a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder; standard karyotype cannot reliably detect it — molecular testing is mandatory.

clinicalStem: A 9-year-old boy presents for evaluation of skin lesions. His mother noticed multiple brown patches on his trunk since infancy. On examination he has 8 flat hyperpigmented macules on the trunk, the largest measuring 2 cm. He also has freckling in both axillae. Slit-lamp examination reveals 4 Lisch nodules on the left iris and 3 on the right. His father has similar skin lesions. Neurological examination is normal. What is the MOST LIKELY diagnosis?
explanation: NF1 diagnosis requires 2 or more of the defined criteria: ≥6 café-au-lait macules (>5 mm prepubertal), axillary or inguinal freckling (Crowe's sign), ≥2 Lisch nodules, ≥2 neurofibromas, optic glioma, distinctive osseous lesion, or first-degree relative with NF1. This boy has ≥6 café-au-lait macules, axillary freckling, Lisch nodules, AND an affected first-degree relative — meeting 4 criteria.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — this patient meets at least 4 NIH diagnostic criteria for NF1 (CALM, axillary freckling, Lisch nodules, affected parent).
McCune-Albright has large café-au-lait macules with irregular ('coast of Maine') borders, precocious puberty, and polyostotic fibrous dysplasia — not this picture.
Tuberous sclerosis presents with ash-leaf hypopigmented macules, shagreen patches, periungual fibromas, and cortical tubers; Lisch nodules and axillary freckling are not features.
NF2 presents with bilateral vestibular schwannomas; café-au-lait macules and Lisch nodules are uncommon in NF2.
Isolated café-au-lait macules (fewer than 6, no other features) may be benign, but this patient has additional features (freckling, Lisch nodules, family history) that confirm NF1.
clinicalPearl: NF1 diagnosis requires ≥2 NIH criteria; the triad of ≥6 CALM + axillary freckling + Lisch nodules is virtually pathognomonic.
keyClue: ≥6 café-au-lait macules + axillary freckling + Lisch nodules + affected first-degree relative.
commonExamTrap: Counting only CALM and missing that axillary freckling and Lisch nodules are independent diagnostic criteria for NF1.
missedConcept: NF1 is diagnosed clinically using NIH criteria; Lisch nodules (iris hamartomas) are highly specific and found in >90% of NF1 patients over age 6.

clinicalStem: A 14-month-old girl presents with infantile spasms. On examination her skin shows three hypopigmented leaf-shaped macules visible under Wood's lamp. Her mother mentions that she has small red papules on the nose and cheeks that appeared recently. EEG confirms hypsarrhythmia. Which imaging modality is MOST APPROPRIATE as the next investigation to evaluate the intracranial manifestations of her condition?
options: Brain MRI with and without gadolinium
explanation: Tuberous sclerosis complex (TSC) is strongly suggested by ash-leaf hypopigmented macules (best seen on Wood's lamp), facial angiofibromas ('adenoma sebaceum'), and infantile spasms with hypsarrhythmia. Brain MRI is the imaging modality of choice for TSC as it best demonstrates cortical tubers, subependymal nodules, and subependymal giant cell astrocytomas (SEGA) without ionising radiation.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — Brain MRI is superior for detecting cortical tubers and subependymal nodules and avoids radiation in a young child.
CT can detect calcified subependymal nodules but is inferior to MRI for cortical tubers and exposes the infant to ionising radiation.
Skull X-ray has no role in evaluating intracranial tuberous sclerosis lesions.
PET scan is not a first-line investigation; it may identify epileptogenic tubers in pre-surgical evaluation but is not the initial diagnostic step.
Transcranial Doppler assesses cerebrovascular flow and has no role in diagnosing TSC intracranial lesions.
clinicalPearl: In a child with infantile spasms, ash-leaf macules (Wood's lamp-positive), and facial angiofibromas, tuberous sclerosis is the diagnosis; Brain MRI is the best imaging investigation.
keyClue: Ash-leaf hypopigmented macules on Wood's lamp + facial angiofibromas + infantile spasms/hypsarrhythmia.
commonExamTrap: Choosing CT over MRI — CT detects calcified nodules but MRI is far superior for cortical tubers and avoids radiation in an infant.
missedConcept: Ash-leaf macules may be the first skin sign of TSC in infancy and are best seen under Wood's (ultraviolet) lamp; MRI brain is the imaging investigation of choice.

clinicalStem: A 3-year-old boy is brought for evaluation of recurrent focal seizures affecting his right side. His parents report that he was born with a dark red birthmark covering his left forehead, upper eyelid, and cheek. On examination the port-wine birthmark is present in the V1 and V2 trigeminal distributions. Ophthalmology has noted elevated intraocular pressure in the left eye. Which investigation would BEST demonstrate the intracranial vascular abnormality responsible for his seizures?
explanation: Sturge-Weber syndrome is characterised by a facial port-wine stain (V1 distribution), ipsilateral leptomeningeal angioma, and glaucoma. Brain MRI with gadolinium is the investigation of choice to demonstrate the leptomeningeal angiomatosis (pial enhancement), underlying cortical atrophy, and cerebral calcifications. It also avoids radiation and provides superior soft-tissue detail compared with CT.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — gadolinium-enhanced brain MRI demonstrates pial angioma enhancement, cortical atrophy, and is the gold standard imaging for Sturge-Weber.
Non-contrast CT can show the characteristic 'tram-track' calcifications but misses the leptomeningeal angioma and early changes; it is inferior to MRI and uses radiation.
EEG is useful for seizure characterisation and lateralisation but does not image the vascular abnormality.
Cerebral angiography (DSA) is invasive, not first-line; it may show reduced cortical venous drainage but is not routinely used for initial diagnosis.
Skull X-ray tram-track calcifications are a classic finding but appear late, are not present in young children, and do not show the vascular anatomy.
clinicalPearl: In Sturge-Weber, port-wine stain in V1 distribution + focal seizures + glaucoma — gadolinium brain MRI is the best initial neuroimaging test (shows pial enhancement).
keyClue: Port-wine stain in V1/V2 distribution + ipsilateral focal seizures + raised intraocular pressure in a child.
commonExamTrap: Choosing skull X-ray for tram-track calcifications — this is a classic teaching sign but appears late and MRI is far superior for initial evaluation.
missedConcept: The leptomeningeal angioma in Sturge-Weber is best seen with gadolinium MRI as pial enhancement; CT calcifications ('tram track') appear in older children and are a late finding.

clinicalStem: A 2-year-old boy born at 28 weeks gestation presents to the developmental paediatrics clinic. His mother reports that he is not walking yet and has stiff legs. On examination he has hypertonia with increased deep tendon reflexes in both lower limbs, scissoring gait on assisted ambulation, and brisk ankle clonus bilaterally. Upper limb tone and cognition appear relatively preserved. Cranial nerve examination is normal. Which type of cerebral palsy BEST describes his motor impairment?
explanation: Spastic diplegia is the most common form of CP in preterm infants and is characterised by bilateral lower limb spasticity with relative sparing of the upper limbs. It results from periventricular leukomalacia (PVL), which preferentially damages the corticospinal tracts for the lower limbs running adjacent to the lateral ventricles. The scissoring gait, lower limb hyperreflexia, and clonus with preserved upper limbs and cognition are classic.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — bilateral lower limb spasticity with upper limb sparing in an ex-premature infant is the hallmark of spastic diplegia.
Spastic hemiplegia affects one side of the body (arm and leg ipsilaterally) — both lower limbs are equally affected here.
Dyskinetic CP features involuntary movements (athetosis, dystonia), variable tone, and no true spasticity or hyperreflexia pattern.
Spastic quadriplegia affects all four limbs with equal or greater upper limb involvement and is typically associated with more severe hypoxic-ischaemic injury.
Ataxic CP features incoordination, hypotonia, and intention tremor — not spasticity or hyperreflexia.
clinicalPearl: Spastic diplegia in an ex-preterm infant = periventricular leukomalacia; bilateral lower limb spasticity + scissoring + preserved upper limbs is the classic pattern.
keyClue: 28-week preterm + bilateral lower limb spasticity and hyperreflexia + scissoring gait + preserved upper limbs.
commonExamTrap: Choosing spastic quadriplegia — in quadriplegia all four limbs are significantly affected; here the upper limbs are relatively spared.
missedConcept: PVL in preterm infants selectively damages lower limb fibres in the corticospinal tract, explaining the predominant lower limb involvement in spastic diplegia.

clinicalStem: A 3-year-old girl with established spastic hemiplegia (right side) is being investigated for the underlying aetiology of her cerebral palsy. She was born at term via normal vaginal delivery with no perinatal asphyxia. The paediatrician wants to identify any structural brain abnormality contributing to her presentation. She is clinically stable. Which single investigation is MOST APPROPRIATE to define the neuroanatomical cause?
options: Brain MRI (without sedation using HASTE sequences)
explanation: Brain MRI is the recommended investigation to determine the aetiology of cerebral palsy because it provides superior soft-tissue contrast resolution to identify cortical malformations, periventricular leukomalacia, focal infarcts, or other structural abnormalities without ionising radiation. Fast sequences (HASTE/SSFSE) can minimise the need for sedation in young children. MRI identifies a structural cause in up to 80% of CP cases.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — MRI is the gold-standard neuroimaging investigation for cerebral palsy aetiology, recommended by international guidelines.
CT exposes the child to ionising radiation and is inferior to MRI for cortical and white matter detail; it is used mainly when MRI is unavailable or in the acute setting.
EEG characterises epileptiform activity and seizure type but does not delineate the structural brain abnormality causing hemiplegia.
Transcranial ultrasound is useful in the neonatal period (open fontanelle) for PVL and IVH but is inadequate for detailed cortical anatomy in a 3-year-old.
Skull X-ray provides no useful information about brain parenchyma or CP aetiology.
clinicalPearl: Brain MRI is the investigation of choice for determining the aetiology of cerebral palsy; it identifies a structural cause in ~80% of cases and guides prognosis.
keyClue: Established spastic hemiplegia at 3 years, term birth, clinically stable — investigate structural aetiology with neuroimaging.
commonExamTrap: Ordering CT instead of MRI — CT is quicker but inferior for cortical/white matter detail and involves ionising radiation in a child.
missedConcept: MRI is recommended over CT for CP aetiology workup; it identifies cortical malformations, focal infarcts, and PVL that CT may miss.

sourceLecture: Developmental Problems & Child With Special Needs
sourceFile: Developmental Problems & Child With Special Needs.pdf
clinicalStem: An 18-month-old boy is referred for developmental concerns. His parents report that he does not respond to his name, has stopped saying the two words he had at 12 months, does not point to objects, does not imitate actions, and has poor eye contact. He lines up toys repeatedly instead of engaging in pretend play. His hearing test is normal. Which is the MOST APPROPRIATE next step to formally evaluate his developmental profile?
options: Structured developmental assessment using ADOS-2 and ADI-R
Brain MRI to exclude structural pathology
Referral for speech therapy only and review in 6 months
explanation: This child demonstrates the red flags for autism spectrum disorder (ASD): loss of language (regression), absent pointing, poor eye contact, no response to name, and restricted repetitive behaviours. The Autism Diagnostic Observation Schedule (ADOS-2) and Autism Diagnostic Interview-Revised (ADI-R) are the gold-standard diagnostic instruments for ASD. Formal structured assessment is the mandatory next step after clinical suspicion.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — ADOS-2 and ADI-R are the gold-standard assessments for diagnosing autism spectrum disorder and should be arranged as soon as possible after red flags are identified.
Brain MRI is not routinely indicated in ASD without neurological signs (focal deficits, regression with abnormal EEG, or neurocutaneous features); it will not diagnose ASD.
Chromosomal microarray may be performed as part of the ASD workup to identify a genetic cause, but the immediate next step is formal developmental/autism assessment, not genetic testing.
Lead toxicity can cause developmental regression and behavioural changes but would not explain absent pointing, poor eye contact, and the full ASD profile; hearing is already normal.
Watchful waiting and speech therapy alone delays formal diagnosis and intervention; ASD requires a structured multidisciplinary assessment without unnecessary delay.
clinicalPearl: Language regression + absent pointing + poor eye contact + repetitive play in an 18-month-old = autism red flags; refer immediately for structured ASD assessment (ADOS-2/ADI-R).
keyClue: Language regression + absent joint attention (no pointing) + poor eye contact + repetitive behaviours in an 18-month-old.
commonExamTrap: Deferring to speech therapy alone — ASD is a multidisciplinary diagnosis requiring formal assessment; speech therapy is one component of management, not the diagnostic step.
missedConcept: Language regression after 12 months is always a red flag requiring urgent evaluation; combined with absent pointing and poor eye contact, ASD assessment is the immediate priority.

sourceLecture: Developmental Problems & Child With Special Needs
sourceFile: Developmental Problems & Child With Special Needs.pdf
clinicalStem: A 7-year-old boy is referred by his school. Teachers report that over the past 18 months he has been unable to sit still in class, frequently interrupts others, cannot wait his turn, forgets to hand in homework, and loses his belongings repeatedly. His parents confirm similar behaviour at home for over a year. He has no learning disability on formal testing. Neurological examination is normal. Vision and hearing are intact. Which diagnosis BEST fits this clinical picture?
options: Attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), combined presentation
explanation: ADHD combined presentation (DSM-5) requires ≥6 symptoms of inattention AND ≥6 symptoms of hyperactivity-impulsivity, present in ≥2 settings for ≥6 months, before age 12, causing functional impairment. This boy has inattention (forgets homework, loses things), hyperactivity (can't sit still), and impulsivity (interrupts, can't wait turn) — present at school AND home for >1 year, in a 7-year-old with normal intelligence.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — the full DSM-5 criteria for ADHD combined type are met: inattention + hyperactivity-impulsivity in two settings for >6 months before age 12.
Conduct disorder involves persistent violation of rules and the rights of others (aggression, theft, deceit) — not the primary picture here.
ASD involves social communication deficits and restricted/repetitive behaviours; while ADHD and ASD can co-occur, the core ASD features are not described here.
Anxiety disorder can impair attention and cause restlessness, but the primary features (impulsivity, hyperactivity, inattention across settings) are more consistent with ADHD.
Specific learning disability primarily affects academic skills (reading, maths) — formal testing here is normal and the behavioural profile points to ADHD.
clinicalPearl: ADHD diagnosis requires symptoms in ≥2 settings for ≥6 months, onset before age 12, and impairment in functioning; combined type = inattention + hyperactivity-impulsivity.
keyClue: Inattention + hyperactivity + impulsivity present both at home AND school for >12 months in a 7-year-old with normal cognition.
commonExamTrap: Attributing the behaviour to conduct disorder — conduct disorder involves deliberate rule violations and aggression, not the inattention and forgetfulness profile seen here.
missedConcept: ADHD must be present in ≥2 settings to exclude environment-specific problems; both home and school reports are essential for diagnosis.

clinicalStem: A 6-week-old boy is referred after his newborn screening (Guthrie test at day 5) showed an elevated phenylalanine level. He has been exclusively breastfed and is feeding well. On examination he has fair skin, blue eyes, and blond hair compared to his darker-complexioned parents. He has no dysmorphic features and appears well. Urine has a musty odour. Which investigation would BEST confirm the underlying metabolic diagnosis?
options: Plasma amino acid quantification (serum phenylalanine and tyrosine ratio)
explanation: Phenylketonuria (PKU) is confirmed by demonstrating elevated plasma phenylalanine (>1000 µmol/L) with a markedly elevated phenylalanine:tyrosine ratio on quantitative plasma amino acid analysis. This distinguishes classic PKU from hyperphenylalaninaemia and from BH4-deficiency variants. The clinical picture (fair skin/hair in a dark family, musty urine, elevated newborn screen) is classic for PKU.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — quantitative plasma amino acid analysis measuring phenylalanine and the Phe:Tyr ratio is the definitive confirmatory test for PKU.
Urine organic acids identify organic acidaemias (propionic, methylmalonic); phenylalanine does not appear as an abnormal organic acid; this is not the right test.
Urine reducing substances screen for galactosaemia and fructosaemia; they have no role in confirming PKU.
Thyroid function tests would be indicated if neonatal hypothyroidism were suspected (jaundice, lethargy, hypotonia); fair colouring and musty urine point to PKU, not hypothyroidism.
Skin biopsy for enzyme analysis is not used to diagnose PKU; plasma amino acid analysis and molecular (PAH gene) testing are the standard approaches.
clinicalPearl: Elevated newborn screening Phe + fair skin/hair in dark family + musty urine = PKU until proven otherwise; confirm with quantitative plasma amino acid analysis (Phe:Tyr ratio).
keyClue: Elevated newborn screening phenylalanine + fair skin and hair in a dark-complexioned family + musty urine odour.
commonExamTrap: Ordering urine organic acids instead of plasma amino acids — organic acidurias are a different metabolic group; PKU is an aminoacidopathy confirmed by plasma amino acid quantification.
missedConcept: Plasma quantitative amino acid analysis is the confirmatory test for PKU; urine phenylalanine derivatives (phenylpyruvate) on urine organic acids will be elevated but the Phe:Tyr ratio on plasma amino acids is the diagnostic gold standard.

sourceLecture: Developmental Problems & Child With Special Needs
sourceFile: Developmental Problems & Child With Special Needs.pdf
clinicalStem: A 3-year-old girl is referred for global developmental delay (GDD). She is delayed in all four domains: motor, language, social, and cognitive. She has no regression, no dysmorphic features on initial assessment, normal cranial nerve exam, and normal neurological findings. Her parents are non-consanguineous. Thyroid function and metabolic screen (lactate, ammonia, urine organic/amino acids) are all normal. Which single genetic investigation currently offers the HIGHEST diagnostic yield as the first-line test in this clinical setting?
options: Fluorescence in situ hybridisation (FISH) for 22q11 deletion
explanation: Chromosomal microarray (array CGH) is currently the first-line genetic investigation for unexplained global developmental delay and intellectual disability, with a diagnostic yield of approximately 15–20%. It detects submicroscopic copy number variants (deletions and duplications) that are missed by standard karyotyping. International guidelines (ACMG) recommend microarray as the first genetic test in children with unexplained GDD/intellectual disability.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — chromosomal microarray has the highest first-line diagnostic yield (~15–20%) for unexplained GDD and is recommended by international guidelines as the first genetic test.
Standard karyotype detects large chromosomal abnormalities (aneuploidy, large rearrangements) but has a diagnostic yield of only ~3–5% and misses submicroscopic CNVs detected by microarray.

FMR1 testing is targeted at Fragile X syndrome, which is more likely in males with specific dysmorphic features; it is reasonable as a second-line or co-ordered test but not the highest-yield first-line test in a non-dysmorphic girl.
Whole exome sequencing (WES) has a high yield (~30–40%) but is expensive, complex to interpret, and is recommended after microarray is negative; it is not the first-line investigation.
FISH for 22q11 deletion is a targeted test for DiGeorge/velocardiofacial syndrome (conotruncal defects, palatal abnormalities, hypocalcaemia); without these features, targeted FISH has a low yield.
clinicalPearl: Chromosomal microarray is the first-line genetic investigation for unexplained GDD/intellectual disability, offering ~15–20% diagnostic yield by detecting submicroscopic CNVs invisible to standard karyotype.
keyClue: Unexplained global developmental delay in all four domains + normal metabolic screen + no dysmorphic features = chromosomal microarray is first-line.
commonExamTrap: Ordering standard karyotype instead of microarray — karyotype only detects large rearrangements (yield 3–5%), while microarray detects submicroscopic CNVs with a much higher yield.
missedConcept: Standard karyotype and microarray are complementary but not equivalent; microarray supersedes karyotype as the first-line test for unexplained GDD because it detects far smaller chromosomal imbalances.
